content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

December 5, 1997


William Seligmann
City Attorney
City of Campbell
3½ N. Santa Cruz Avenue, Suite A
Los Gatos, California 95030

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-565

Dear Mr. Seligmann:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May Councilmember Dean participate in the decision to approve or deny an application
for the development of an office building and parking garage?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Dean may participate in the decision to approve or deny the application
for the office building and parking garage if there is no reasonably foreseeable and material
economic effect on Compuware, his source of income.

FACTS

You are requesting advice concerning the participation of Campbell City Councilmember
Matthew Dean on a development application. The application seeks a special development
permit for the development of a six story office building and parking structure, as well as a use
permit for a hotel expansion on a parcel commonly known as the Pruneyard. Mr. Dean is


¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-565
Page 2

employed by a NASDAQ company known as Compuware. Compuware presently rents space at
an existing office building in the Pruneyard, and is anticipated to be one of the primary tenants in
the new office building.

The final decision on the new office building and parking structure rests with the city
council. The final decision on the hotel expansion rests with the city planning commission, but
can be appealed to the city council. However, according to your phone call on December 3,
1997, the city planning commission has already made its decision and the time period for an
appeal has lapsed. Therefore, your question regarding this decision is moot and will not be
addressed.

ANALYSIS

Introduction

The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to insure that public officials perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who support them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

Economic Interests

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on, among other things, any source of income of $250 or more in a twelve month
period or any business entity in which the public official is employed. Mr. Dean is an employee
of Compuware and receives $250 or more in income from Compuware. Therefore, Mr. Dean has
an economic interest in Compuware.

Foreseeability

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) According to your facts, Compuware will be moving into
the new six story building, if it is built. This move will certainly have some financial effect on
Compuware. Therefore, we find that the foreseeability standard is met.

File No. A-97-565
Page 3

Materiality

Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is still only required where the foreseeable
effect on the public official's economic interest is material. The Commission has adopted a
series of regulations that provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effects
of a decision are material. (Regulation 18702.) The standard of materiality differs depending on
the type of economic interest involved and whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in a decision.²

Compuware, a business entity, is only indirectly involved in the decision whether to
allow construction of the new building and parking structure. Therefore, we go to Regulation
18702.2 for the applicable standard. The precise standard depends on the size of the business
entity indirectly affected. Since Compuware is traded over NASDAQ, subsection (b) of
Regulation 18702.2 provides the applicable standard:

"(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
gross revenues for a fiscal year of $150,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more;
(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of assets or liabilities of $150,000 or more."

If the materiality standard is met, then Mr. Dean may not participate in any governmental
decisions regarding the six story building and the parking garage. The Commission does not act
as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore,
Mr. Dean must make the final decision whether the materiality standard is met.

2        "A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agency when that person, either personally or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business
entity." (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

File No. A-97-565
Page 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Marte Castañios
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:MC:tls

CORRECTED LETTER: 02/04/98

(PAGE 2, PARAGRAPH 4; CHANGED OVER $250 TO $250 OR MORE)

J:\97ltrsa\97565.wpd

May Councilmember Dean participate in the decision to approve or deny an application
for the development of an office building and parking garage?

for the office building and parking garage if there is no reasonably foreseeable and material
economic effect on Compuware, his source of income.

for the development of an office building and parking garage?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Dean may participate in the decision to approve or deny the application
for the office building and parking garage if there is no reasonably foreseeable and material
